current_fact.fact_text: In 1794, Sardinian lawyer Giovanni Maria Angioy led an uprising against the rule of the House of Savoy, driving the viceroy and his officials out of Cagliari. Though ultimately crushed, the revolt planted enduring seeds of Sardinian autonomy and identity that still echo on the island.

episode.title: Morning Drops #18 - Sardegna
episode.description: Yasmin traces her connection to Sardegna — the island where her Italian journey began. Through music and memory, she shares the story of how this place shaped her family's path, and why it still holds such a deep place in her heart.
songs: Mahmood & Tenores di Bitti - Com’è profondo il mare

current_fact.match_reason: A Sardinian revolution for self-determination is a perfect historical mirror for an episode rooted in Sardegna’s landscape, identity, and music.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1625, a combined Spanish and Portuguese fleet of 52 ships began the recapture of Bahia in Brazil from Dutch control. The amphibious campaign, involving European powers fighting over a distant colonial port, reveals how early global trade routes turned coastal cities into chess pieces of empire.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A colonial-era naval campaign over a Latin American port links to an episode about movement, travel, and world/Latin-inflected sounds, reframing conquest-era routes as reflective journeys.

queue.fact_text: In 1941, the Ustaše regime carried out the Gudovac massacre, killing nearly 200 Serbs in a village near Bjelovar. It was the first mass killing in a genocidal campaign in the Independent State of Croatia, a grim signal of how rapidly extremist ideology can turn neighbors into targets.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: A Serbian-targeted massacre rooted in extremist belief contrasts with an episode about rethinking assumptions—including a Serbian musician—inviting listeners to challenge ideologies before they turn deadly.

queue.fact_text: In 1949, former Philippine First Lady Aurora Quezon was ambushed and killed while traveling to inaugurate a hospital named after her late husband. Her assassination, blamed on the communist-aligned Hukbalahap movement, stunned a country that had known her as a symbol of social welfare and quiet dignity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: The abrupt loss of a public figure en route to a hospital dedication invites a reflective, grief-aware pairing with an episode about sudden change, vulnerability, and slow healing.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1937, South African researcher Max Theiler created the yellow fever vaccine at the Rockefeller Foundation in New York, turning a once-deadly scourge into a preventable disease. His work, based on carefully weakening the virus in lab conditions, laid crucial groundwork for modern virology and vaccine design.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A vaccine born from careful experimentation and boundary-pushing science matches an episode about a genre built on hybridization, sampling, and quietly reshaping what music could be.

queue.fact_text: In 1975, as North Vietnamese forces closed in on Saigon, General Cao Văn Viên—the last chief of staff of the South Vietnamese army—departed for the United States. His flight symbolized not just a personal exile, but the imminent collapse of an entire state and the end of a long, devastating war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: The poignant end of a regime and a war fits with an episode about transitions, storms passing, and the uneasy hope that 'it can't rain forever.'

queue.fact_text: In 1994, former CIA counterintelligence officer Aldrich Ames pleaded guilty to spying for the Soviet Union and later Russia. Over nine years, his betrayal exposed dozens of American agents, some of whom were executed, making him one of the most damaging spies in U.S. history.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: A double life as a spy reflects the theme of multiple identities and hidden selves explored through musical alter-egos in this episode.

queue.fact_text: In 1996, amid the Whitewater controversy, U.S. President Bill Clinton gave a four-and-a-half-hour videotaped deposition—the first time a sitting president testified as a witness in a criminal trial. The slow, methodical questioning captured on tape highlights how modern media can turn legal complexity into a strange form of public spectacle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A media-saturated political scandal being shaped in the public eye parallels how Auto-Tune transformed and 'managed' voices, raising questions about authenticity and control.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1965, as the Dominican Civil War unfolded, U.S. troops landed claiming to prevent a communist dictatorship, marking one of the largest American military interventions in Latin America after World War II. The operation blurred lines between evacuation mission and regime-change, leaving a complicated legacy in Dominican history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: A military intervention framed as protective power contrasts with an episode about disciplined, embodied forms of combat (Muay Thai, boxing), inviting a different perspective on force and control.

queue.fact_text: In 1991, Space Shuttle Discovery lifted off on STS-39 carrying a suite of instruments that quietly mapped Earth’s atmosphere and tested new surveillance technologies. It was the first time a Department of Defense shuttle mission was officially unclassified, blurring the line between military hardware and open scientific exploration.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A mission that opens military space work to public view pairs well with the launch of a new, drifting 'Evening Flow' format—both invite audiences into previously hidden journeys.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1941, yellow fever vaccine pioneer Max Theiler’s work began to show its broad impact as mass vaccination campaigns took root, turning once-feared outbreaks into controllable threats. The story of yellow fever—from mysterious 'black vomit' fevers to a laboratory-attenuated virus—reads like a medical detective tale across continents and mosquitoes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A global health story shaped by tiny mosquitoes is paired with an episode about immense marine animals and fragile ecosystems, connecting different scales of nature, risk, and awe.

published.fact_text: In 1991, Space Shuttle Discovery lifted off on mission STS-39, the first U.S. Department of Defense shuttle flight that wasn’t shrouded in secrecy. Instead of classified experiments, its payload bay carried unclassified instruments studying Earth’s atmosphere and testing advanced sensors, quietly marking a shift toward more open collaboration between military tech and scientific research.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A space-themed shuttle mission pairs naturally with an episode built around 'Space Shift' and expansive, forward-looking energy.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1973, Pink Floyd’s "The Dark Side of the Moon" reached number one on the U.S. Billboard chart, beginning an almost unbelievable 741-week run on the album chart. Its blend of studio experimentation, conceptual storytelling, and lush sound design quietly redefined what a rock album could be.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: The fact is literally about Pink Floyd and studio experimentation, and this episode not only features Pink Floyd but frames listening as a cosmic voyage.

published.fact_text: In 1937, South African–American virologist Max Theiler developed the first effective vaccine against yellow fever at the Rockefeller Foundation in New York. His painstaking work with weakened virus strains dramatically reduced a disease that had once terrorized port cities and tropical regions, earning him a Nobel Prize and saving countless lives.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: Developing a vaccine is a masterclass in persistence and experimentation, perfectly echoing an episode about the joy and struggle of learning over time.

published.fact_text: In 1947, Norwegian explorer Thor Heyerdahl and five crew members left Peru on a balsa wood raft named Kon-Tiki to test a radical idea: that ancient South Americans could have reached Polynesia using simple rafts and ocean currents. For 101 days they drifted across the Pacific, turning a controversial hypothesis into one of history’s most audacious real-life experiments.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A bold ocean raft crossing fits naturally with a sea-bound episode about life on a boat, shared stories, and moving with the waves.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1869, Chinese and Irish laborers working for the Central Pacific Railroad laid a staggering ten miles of track in a single day on the first U.S. transcontinental railroad. This feat of coordination and endurance has never been surpassed, yet the names of the workers who achieved it were largely left out of the official celebration.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

published.match_reason: An industrial, steel-and-rails miracle aligns with a 'Steel City' episode steeped in industrial textures and stories of common people behind big structures.

published.fact_text: In 1789, sailors aboard HMS Bounty mutinied against Lieutenant William Bligh in the South Pacific, setting him and 18 loyal crew adrift in a small open boat. Against the odds, Bligh navigated more than 3,600 nautical miles to safety, while the mutineers vanished toward remote islands, leaving a tangle of legend, survival, and fractured loyalties.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A sea mutiny and epic open-boat voyage connect with an episode about immersion, water, and going below the surface in both ocean and emotion.

published.fact_text: In 1944, during Exercise Tiger—the secret rehearsal for the D-Day landings—nine German E-boats attacked Allied landing ships off the English coast. Nearly 950 American and British servicemen were killed in a catastrophe so sensitive that survivors were ordered to stay silent, hiding one of World War II’s deadliest training disasters for years.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A hidden wartime tragedy and the violence of training for invasion align with an episode centered on political anger, rupture, and the costs of conflict.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1986, abnormally high radiation levels were detected at Sweden’s Forsmark Nuclear Power Plant—over 1,000 kilometers from Chernobyl. The alarm in Sweden forced Soviet authorities to admit that a catastrophic nuclear accident had occurred, revealing a disaster that might otherwise have stayed hidden for much longer.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A nuclear disaster discovered indirectly mirrors how hidden systems affect our lives—much like invisible digital overload and the unseen infrastructures explored in 'Nomophobia.'

published.fact_text: In 1948, Igor Stravinsky conducted the American premiere of his ballet "Orpheus" at New York City Center, reimagining the ancient myth through cool, neoclassical lines. Instead of fiery drama, his Orpheus is introspective and restrained, proving that even a story about love and loss in the underworld can be told with delicate, modern clarity.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

published.match_reason: The Orpheus myth is a quintessential love story of loss and vulnerability, resonating with an episode dedicated to love's most fragile, artistic side.

published.fact_text: In 2004, CBS News broadcast photographs from Abu Ghraib prison showing U.S. soldiers torturing and humiliating Iraqi detainees. The images shattered official narratives about the war, igniting global outrage and forcing a painful confrontation with questions of power, cruelty, and accountability.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A shocking exposure of abuse aligns with an episode about an artist whose work fiercely interrogates power, injustice, and the hidden violence of institutions.

published.fact_text: In 1965, U.S. troops landed in the Dominican Republic under the claim of preventing a communist takeover during the country’s civil war. The intervention, which quickly escalated into a large-scale occupation, remains a deeply controversial moment in Cold War history and Dominican memory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: A Caribbean intervention and Cold War tensions are paired with an episode that explores Latin and Caribbean musical histories, politics, and cultural power from the region’s own perspective.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1952, Japan and the Republic of China signed the Treaty of Taipei, formally ending the Second Sino-Japanese War between them. The treaty quietly redrew legal lines over territories, reparations, and sovereignty in East Asia, even as larger geopolitical disputes around those same issues continued to smolder.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A peace treaty that tries to slow the momentum of war fits a 'Slow Planet' episode about pause, de-escalation, and gentler rhythms after turbulence.

published.fact_text: In 1796, the Armistice of Cherasco was signed between Napoleon Bonaparte and King Victor Amadeus III of Sardinia, forcing Sardinia to cede key fortresses and territory along the Mediterranean. It marked an early step in Napoleon’s transformation from ambitious general into a force reshaping the map of Europe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: A treaty reshaping northern Italian territories matches an Italian-focused episode exploring the contrasts and tensions between different parts of the country.

published.fact_text: In 1794, Sardinian lawyer Giovanni Maria Angioy led an uprising against the rule of the House of Savoy, driving the viceroy and his officials out of Cagliari. Though ultimately crushed, the revolt planted enduring seeds of Sardinian autonomy and identity that still echo on the island.

episode.title: Morning Drops #18 - Sardegna
episode.description: Yasmin traces her connection to Sardegna — the island where her Italian journey began. Through music and memory, she shares the story of how this place shaped her family's path, and why it still holds such a deep place in her heart.
songs: Mahmood & Tenores di Bitti - Com’è profondo il mare

published.match_reason: A Sardinian revolution for self-determination is a perfect historical mirror for an episode rooted in Sardegna’s landscape, identity, and music.
